name: Silver Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Silver. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power range for Silver cleaning considering thermal conductivity

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Silver's moderate reflectivity and thermal processing

spotSize.description: Optimal spot size for precision cleaning of Silver surfaces

repetitionRate.description: High repetition rate for efficient cleaning while managing heat accumulation

energyDensity.description: Fluence optimized for Silver ablation threshold and cleaning effectiveness

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation without excessive thermal damage

scanSpeed.description: Scan speed balancing cleaning efficiency and process control

passCount.description: Number of passes for complete contaminant removal without substrate damage

overlapRatio.description: Optimal overlap between adjacent scan lines for uniform cleaning

power.description: Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaning of reflective metals like silver; starting at 100W to account for high reflectivity (~98% at 1064 nm) reducing absorption; max limited to avoid melting (Tm=962°C); based on IPG Photonics and Laserax equipment specs for metal surface preparation.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal on silver; 2000 mm/s recommended for optimal balance of cleaning rate and minimal heat accumulation due to silver's high thermal conductivity (429 W/m·K); lower for heavy contamination, higher for light; from industry manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems) and process optimization studies.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of silver with low heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise applications but unnecessary for industrial contamination removal; ns avoids plasma shielding issues at 1064 nm; supported by laser physics research (e.g., J. Appl. Phys. studies on metal ablation).

repetition_rate.description: Standard for pulsed laser cleaning; 50 kHz recommended for silver to enable fast processing while limiting heat buildup from pulse overlap; higher rates increase speed but risk thermal damage on reflective surfaces; trade-off noted in academic literature (e.g., Opt. Lasers Eng. reviews on pulse train effects).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe removal of contaminants (e.g., oxides, fingerprints) on silver; below ablation threshold (~2.5 J/cm² for ns pulses at 1064 nm due to high reflectivity); 1.0 J/cm² recommended to minimize substrate damage; from ablation threshold studies (e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. papers on silver at IR wavelengths).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for industrial cleaning; 200 μm recommended for silver to provide good resolution on detailed surfaces while covering larger areas efficiently; smaller for fine features, larger for broad preparation; per equipment specifications (e.g., CleanLaser CL series manuals).

pass_count.description: Typically 1-3 passes for light contamination on silver, up to 5 for heavy buildup; multi-pass (2 recommended) ensures complete removal without exceeding thermal limits; single pass possible for very light dirt; drawn from industry case studies (e.g., laser cleaning of cultural heritage silver artifacts in J. Cult. Herit.).

overlap_ratio.description: Standard overlap for uniform coverage in raster scanning; 70% recommended for silver to prevent streaking from high reflectivity and ensure even fluence distribution; lower (50%) for speed on large areas, higher (80%) for precision; from process optimization studies (e.g., Opt. Express on scan strategies for metals).
